charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of why Lukasiewicz logic is preferred, the McNaughton characterisation, the tree algorithm, and key supporting lemmas.

nodes.label:
Week 8
McNaughton
nodes.note: Week 8 zooms in on Lukasiewicz logic F3 and proves McNaughton's Theorem, which characterises exactly which functions on [0,1] can be represented by F3 propositional formulas.

nodes.label: Why F3?
nodes.note: F3 is the only one of the three standard fuzzy logics that satisfies the law of excluded middle and lets us define min and max as compound operators.

nodes.label:
Excluded
Middle (LEM)
nodes.note: In F3 the formula p or not-p evaluates to 1 for every value of p. This fails in F1 and F2.

nodes.label:
F3 extends
F1
nodes.note: Inside F3 we can define new operators that compute min and max on truth values, so any reasoning in F1 can be replicated in F3.

nodes.label:
McNaughton
Theorem
nodes.note: A function f : [0,1] -> [0,1] is the truth function of some F3 formula iff it is continuous, piecewise linear, and uses only integer coefficients.

nodes.label: Lemma 8.2
nodes.note: Every truth function of an F3 formula is continuous, piecewise linear, and uses integer coefficients. Proved by induction on formula structure.

nodes.label: Lemma 8.3
nodes.note: For every pair of integers n and m there is an F3 formula whose truth function equals the clamped linear function [nx+m]_0^1.

nodes.label:
Tree
Algorithm
nodes.note: Constructive procedure that turns the recursive proof of Lemma 8.3 into a binary tree which yields the actual formula for any [nx+m]_0^1.

nodes.label:
Wedge
Combination
nodes.note: Once we have formulas for individual clamped linear pieces we combine them using min and max into wedge-shaped functions to realise any continuous piecewise linear target.

nodes.label:
Lemma 8.4
(non-exam)
nodes.note: Sharp approximation lemma using Bezout's identity. Provides the converse direction of McNaughton for arbitrary rational breakpoints.

nodes.label:
Lukasiewicz
Identities
nodes.note: De Morgan laws and the equivalence of implication with not-p or q hold in F3, mirroring classical propositional logic.

nodes.label:
Partition
Lemma
nodes.note: Intersecting two partitions of [0,1] gives another partition. Used to merge the partitions induced by sub-formulas in the proof of Lemma 8.2.

nodes.label:
Clamping
[z]_0^1
nodes.note: The clamping operator [z]_0^1 = min(1, max(0, z)) is the basic building block for representable functions in McNaughton's theorem.

charts.title: Tree Algorithm for [nx+m]_0^1
charts.description: Recursive procedure that constructs an F3 propositional formula whose truth function equals the clamped linear function [nx+m]_0^1.

nodes.label:
Want formula for
[nx + m]_0^1
nodes.note: We start with integer parameters n and m and want to build an F3 propositional formula whose truth function equals the clamped linear function [nx+m]_0^1.

nodes.label: n = 0?
nodes.note: Check whether the slope is zero. If n is zero we have reached a base case and the function is a constant 0 or 1.

nodes.label: m <= 0 ?
nodes.note: Decide whether the constant is below the lower clamp or above the upper clamp. The result is either falsity or tautology.

nodes.label:
Use bottom
(p and not p)
nodes.note: The clamped value is 0, so any contradiction such as p and not p suffices as the formula.

nodes.label:
Use top
(p or not p)
nodes.note: The clamped value is 1, so any tautology such as p or not p suffices as the formula.

nodes.label: n > 0?
nodes.note: Decide which recursive case applies depending on the sign of n. Positive slopes use p; negative slopes use not p.

nodes.label:
Recurse on
(n-1)x+m and (n-1)x+(m+1)
nodes.note: Generate two child sub-problems: the slope decreases by 1, with m unchanged for the left child and incremented by 1 for the right child.

nodes.label:
Combine:
(F_left or p) and F_right
nodes.note: The parent formula is built by disjoining the left child's formula with p and conjoining the result with the right child's formula.

nodes.label:
Recurse on
(n+1)x+(m-1) and (n+1)x+m
nodes.note: For negative n, generate two child sub-problems with n increased by 1, and decremented m for the left child.

nodes.label:
Combine:
(F_left or not p) and F_right
nodes.note: Combine using disjunction with not p instead of p, then conjoin with the right child's formula.

nodes.label:
Output formula
F_n,m
nodes.note: Walk back up the recursion tree, building the parent formula from its two children at each step. The root yields the desired formula.

charts.title: Lemma 8.2 Proof Structure
charts.description: Induction on formula structure showing every F3 truth function is continuous piecewise linear with integer coefficients.

nodes.label:
Formula F
in F3
nodes.note: We want to prove by induction on the size of F that its F3 truth function is continuous, piecewise linear, and has integer coefficients.

nodes.label:
F is a
propositional variable?
nodes.note: Base case of the induction. If F is just p then its truth function is the identity x, which is trivially piecewise linear with integer coefficients.

nodes.label:
Truth function = x
(slope 1, b 0)
nodes.note: The function is a single linear piece with integer slope 1 and intercept 0, so the property holds in the base case.

nodes.label:
Top connective
in F?
nodes.note: Identify the outermost connective of F and apply the corresponding inductive case (negation, conjunction, disjunction, or implication).

nodes.label:
Negation:
use 1 - g(x)
nodes.note: If F = not G, the truth function is 1 - g(x). Replacing each linear piece a*x + b by -a*x + (1-b) keeps integer coefficients.

nodes.label:
Conjunction:
use max(0,g+h-1)
nodes.note: If F = G and H, refine the partitions to a common one and replace each pair of linear pieces by max(0, g(x) + h(x) - 1).

nodes.label:
Disjunction:
de Morgan reduction
nodes.note: If F = G or H, rewrite as not (not G and not H) and reuse the negation and conjunction cases inductively.

nodes.label:
Implication:
rewrite to or
nodes.note: If F = G to H, rewrite as not G or H and reduce to the disjunction case via De Morgan.

nodes.label:
Truth function is
integer piecewise linear
nodes.note: All four cases preserve continuity, piecewise-linearity, and integer coefficients, so by induction the property holds for every F3 formula.

charts.title: Building Formulas for Piecewise-Linear Functions
charts.description: High-level procedure for constructing an F3 formula whose truth function equals an arbitrary continuous piecewise-linear target.

nodes.label:
Target function f
(continuous PL, integer coeffs)
nodes.note: We are given a target function on [0,1] that is continuous, piecewise linear, and uses only integer coefficients - precisely the McNaughton-representable functions.

nodes.label:
Decompose f into
wedges or pieces
nodes.note: Identify the basic linear pieces or wedge shapes that compose f. Each wedge is a function that rises and falls linearly between integer-coefficient lines.

nodes.label: Pieces remaining?
nodes.note: Check whether all pieces have been processed. If yes, combine the formulas; if no, build a formula for the next piece.

nodes.label:
Apply tree algorithm
for each [nx+m]
nodes.note: For each linear piece, use the tree algorithm of Lemma 8.3 to construct an F3 formula whose truth function equals that clamped linear function.

nodes.label:
Combine pair using
underscore-and = min
nodes.note: Pairs of clamped linear formulas can be combined into a wedge by taking their minimum, which is the defined operator underscore-and in F3.

nodes.label:
Combine wedges using
underscore-or = max
nodes.note: Combine all wedges by taking the maximum (defined operator underscore-or). The resulting formula realises the target piecewise linear function.

nodes.label:
Final F3 formula
realises f
nodes.note: All pieces have been combined into a single F3 propositional formula whose truth function is exactly the target f, completing the converse direction of McNaughton's theorem.

charts.title: Why F3 (Lukasiewicz) over F1 and F2
charts.description: Taxonomy of the desirable properties that distinguish Lukasiewicz logic from the other standard fuzzy logics.

nodes.label:
Desirable
properties
nodes.note: We list the properties we want a fuzzy logic to satisfy, then check which of F1, F2, F3 actually delivers them.

nodes.label:
LEM:
p or not-p = 1
nodes.note: We want the law of excluded middle to hold so that the disjunction of any proposition with its negation is fully true.

nodes.label:
Express min
and max as defined
nodes.note: We want to be able to define new operators for min and max inside the logic, so that anything F1 can do can also be done in our chosen logic.

nodes.label: F1 (Min-Max)
nodes.note: Has min and max as primitives but fails LEM: at p = 0.5 the value of p or not-p is just 0.5.

nodes.label: F2 (Product)
nodes.note: Has continuous-to-classical limits but fails LEM: at p = 0.5 the value of p or not-p is 0.75.

nodes.label: F3 (Lukasiewicz)
nodes.note: Satisfies LEM and lets us define min and max via the implication operator. This is why F3 is preferred.

nodes.label:
x and y =
(x to y) and x
nodes.note: Defined conjunction operator that produces min in F3. Allows F3 to simulate F1 inside its own language.

nodes.label:
x or y =
(x to y) to y
nodes.note: Defined disjunction operator that produces max in F3. Together with the defined min, F3 contains an internal copy of F1.
